title: Module 3 quiz: Building a production agent

questions.prompt: What's the canonical production agent loop architecture?

choices.text: Plan-then-react: plan the outline, react within each step. Surface the plan to the user for collaboration; re-plan only when observations contradict the plan. Combines macro structure with micro flexibility — best of both worlds for non-trivial agents.

questions.explanation: Plan-then-react is the production sweet spot for complex agents. Routine queries can drop to pure react; long-running deep tasks can adopt full plan-then-act. The decision is task-shape-aware, not framework-default.

questions.prompt: What's the minimum observability bar for a production agent?

choices.text: Trace ID propagated through every model and tool call; per-call logs (input, output, latency, tokens, cost, errors); persistent storage; UI or query path to inspect traces; replay capability. Multi-step systems are unmaintainable without these.

questions.explanation: Trace IDs are the spine. Per-step logs hold the diagnostic value. Replay is what makes debugging fast. Without the full stack, a 5-minute fix turns into a 5-hour fix.

questions.prompt: An agent ran overnight in a loop, burning a 4-figure bill. What was missing?

choices.text: Layered safety: step cap, per-tool timeouts, wall-clock cap, circuit breakers, cost caps at user/feature/API-key levels. Each catches a different failure mode. Together they make unbounded loops impossible.

questions.explanation: Layered safety controls turn 'unbounded loop' into 'bounded, observable, recoverable.' The API-key spend cap is the airbag — app-level controls catch issues earlier, but the cap stops disasters.

questions.prompt: Your dependency API goes down. Without circuit breakers, what happens?

choices.text: Agents fail fast and skip the dependency.

Every agent run hits the dead API, times out, retries — burning latency and tokens. Circuit breakers fail fast when a dep is sick, return degraded responses, probe periodically to detect recovery. Outages stop cascading.
Nothing — agents are resilient by default.

questions.explanation: Circuit breakers prevent cascading dependency failures. Apply them to external APIs, LLM providers (during outages), and to your agent feature itself when error rates spike.

questions.prompt: What makes a 30-second agent feel responsive?

choices.text: Streaming: surface the plan upfront, stream each tool call ('Looking up...'  '✓ Got result'), stream the final answer as it generates, and show a cancel button. Combined with prompt caching and parallel tool calls, perceived latency drops dramatically.

questions.explanation: Perceived latency drives abandonment, not wall-clock latency. Surfacing intermediate work + streaming output + showing the plan turns a 30-second wait into 30 seconds of visible progress. The agent isn't faster; it feels faster.
